data.title: Aviat Networks: Good Quarter And Constructive Outlook - Buy
data.description:
Note:

I have discussed Aviat Networks, Inc. (AVNW) or “Aviat Networks” previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier coverage of the company.

Earlier this month, leading wireless transport and access solutions provider Aviat Networks reported better-than-expected Q1/FY2026 results, with both sales and profitability coming in ahead of consensus estimates.

Please note that Q1 is a seasonally weaker quarter for the company. Consequently, the sequential decline in sales and profitability should not come as a surprise to investors.

North America continues to be Aviat Networks' largest market:

Free cash flow was impacted by unfavorable working capital movements. The company ended the quarter with $64.8 million in cash and cash equivalents as well as $106.5 million in funded debt. Total liquidity amounted to $136.9 million.

On the conference call, management discussed the company's focus on private networks with strong bookings from public safety and utility customers during the quarter.

The company also highlighted the recent introduction of its Aprisa LTE 5G router solution for police, fire, and emergency vehicles, which opens up a new market for Aviat:

Following a number of analyst questions during the questions-and-answers session of the call, management elaborated on the company's expectations for this new product:

(...) There's a lot of interest in our solution. It will take probably another 6 months, but we think that this is going to be a big growth driver towards the end of this fiscal year going into next fiscal year. (...)

In addition, Aviat Networks has entered the Fixed Wireless Access ("FWA") market with its new multi-dwelling units ("MDUs"):

However, it will likely take several quarters until MDU revenue contributions become more material.

The company also expressed increased optimism regarding its ability to participate in projects funded by the $42.5 billion Broadband Equity Access and Deployment ("BEAD") Program in the second half of next year.

On the flip side, the recent government shutdown could result in some revenue slipping into the third fiscal quarter. However, with the U.S. government only representing approximately 5% of total sales, management does not expect a material impact on Aviat's fiscal year 2026 results.

While the company reaffirmed its outlook for FY2026, management pointed to guidance as being conservative.

At the midpoint of the provided ranges, revenues would grow by approximately 3.5%, while adjusted EBITDA would increase by more than 35%.

Based on management's commentary and assuming solid execution to continue, I would expect FY2026 profitability to come in at the high end of the provided range:

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating on the company's shares with an unchanged price target of $33.50.

Bottom Line:

Aviat Networks reported better-than-expected Q1/FY2026 results and reiterated full-year guidance.

On the call, management highlighted strong demand from private network customers and stated expectations for BEAD funding to commence in the second half of next year.

Given the company's solid start to FY2026 and constructive commentary, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating on the company's shares with an unchanged price target of $33.50.

data.title: Thanksgiving: Top 10 Most Innovative BioPharma Drugs In Phase 3
data.description:
Introduction

As the year comes to an end, we must be thankful for the therapies that are in late clinical stages. Most importantly, patients are set to benefit immensely. But nearly every trial candidate has implications beyond just patients. The approval of a single therapy can create a chain reaction that spreads across the entire healthcare industry. Whether the therapy is the validation of a new pathway or modality to treat our most pressing diseases, all clinical trials are important, but a few stand out for their innovative implications.

In this article, I will discuss the 10 most innovative BioPharma drugs in Phase 3, all between enrollment and approval. There are far more than 10, of course, but I hope the cases I discuss here offer useful insights for investors, patients, and biopharma leaders. They are based on three pillars of innovation and validation: pathway, modality, and best-in-class.

Cemdisiran-pozelimab (cemdi-poze)

  • Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (REGN) and Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY).
  • Mode of Action: RNAi and monoclonal antibody combination therapy targeting the C5 protein.
  • Indications: paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) and myasthenia gravis.

A unique collaboration between biotech stalwarts Regeneron and Alnylam, cemdisiran-pozelimab (cemdi-poze), is a novel strategy for treating C5 protein-implicated diseases. This is a mature field that is highly competitive and validated already. There exist both small molecules and monoclonal antibodies who have all seen various levels of success, including Novartis AG's (NVS) iptacopan and AstraZeneca PLC's (AZN) ravilizumab, as shown below in the chart I made for a class a while ago. Cemdi-poze is looking to disrupt this field by introducing a C5 silencing mechanism alongside reducing C5 in the bloodstream with an antibody. The dual approach may be the necessary approach for patients suffering from severe cases and may even be suitable as a first-line approach if side effects are reduced. The main risk is the development of an siRNA that targets proteins created in the liver, as lipid nanoparticles are the preferred delivery mechanism for oligonucleotides.

However, the space already has one highly competitive member, Chugai Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:CHGCF)/Roche Holding AG's (OTCQX:RHHBY) PiaSky (crovalimab), which highlights the smart benefits of antibody recycling. Essentially, PiaSky is no normal antibody, it is instead engineered to be recycled back into the bloodstream after picking up its target C5 protein payload and depositing it into endosomes for breakdown. Since C5 is a highly expressed component of the complement cascade, PiaSky has the benefit of reduced dosing requirements compared to peers at equal levels of efficacy (AstraZeneca even paid $775 million to Chugai to settle patent infringement issues over recycling technology).

Regeneron and Alnylam must offer superiority over PiaSky, a once-a-month subcutaneous shot, which is a hard task to meet. Already, cemdi-poze is studied as a monthly shot but requires one for each component. This is essentially two shots a month (a single monthly shot is being studied as well). However, the implications of approval are quite significant if patients and physicians favor the dual approach. If superior efficacy is there, this case can support validation of the siRNA/mAb strategy for a wide variety of diseases beyond C5. Therefore, I highly recommend following how cemdi-poze trials and commercialization perform upon its potential approval.

Vepdegestrant

  • Arvinas, Inc. (ARVN) and Pfizer Inc. (PFE).
  • Mechanism of Action: proteolysis-targeting chimera (PROTAC) targeting estrogen receptor (ER).
  • Indication: ER-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer.

Arvinas and Pfizer’s vepdegestrant is the most advanced clinical candidate of the novel PROTAC modality. This new modality is based on an engineered small molecule that performs two functions. One is to bind to a target, in this case ER, and the second is a ligand that binds to an E3 ligase that performs ubiquitination on the target protein. Ubiquitination then leads the target to be picked up and degraded by proteasomes. PROTAC functions are possible due to a special linker segment between the two ligands that allows for the E3 ligase to make contact with the target. This is a significant case of validation for this modality that has the ability to remove faulty proteins throughout a cell that have typically been untreatable or have gained resistance before.

This case has significant implications for the entire healthcare industry. While the leading IP for this technology is tied up with the aforementioned Arvinas and a few other small biotech peers, the possibilities are quite extensive. There are also a few foundational patents as is the case for CRISPR. Therefore, success here will lead to a surge in investment in the modality for a wide variety of targets, particularly those that face high resistance such as oncological proteins like ER, HER2, AR, etc., along with the typically undruggable class of transcription factors such as STAT3, BTK, and IRAK4. Even if vepdegestrant is not a mega blockbuster for Pfizer and Arvinas, an approval will still cause a significant shift in funding and should provide numerous catalysts for decades to come. More on why it may not be a mega blockbuster next.

Giredestrant

  • Roche.
  • Mechanism of Action: Selective Estrogen Receptor Degrader (SERD).
  • Indication: ER-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer.

Many may have read the description of vepdegestrant and thought that it is an immense breakthrough and guaranteed success story, but I recommend caution. The class of drugs is extremely early on and the complex chemical risks of the molecules have prevented squeaky-clean data from emerging so far. While the molecules can be considered far smaller than biologics, the three parts combined do end up larger than is typically safe for dosing. This has led to bioavailability and safety issues, despite PROTAC’s degrading prowess allowing for low doses. Combined with fierce competition in the oncological space, vepdegestrant may be the first mover but is unlikely to stake a lasting claim in the industry.

Therefore, I would like to present another challenger, Roche’s giredestrant. This molecule has the same target and indication but is essentially only one-third of vepdegestrant’s parts: a ligand of ER. PROTACs and SERDs are both in the class of protein degraders, but giredestrant is fortunate enough to provide degradation power without the need to link the molecule to a ligase ligand. However, this is a different case for the power of innovation than the vepdegestrant case as giredestrant is not the first SERD on the market. Instead, the strategy at play is that giredestrant is oral and has shown success in the adjuvant setting, compared to other SERDs on the market that are injections and second-line treatments. This then serves to provide an image of what the full potential of a protein-degrading therapy is once the pharmacological properties and efficacy are optimized fully. In the end, both cases are bullish on the space, but significant serendipity is required before many more molecules enter trials due to the difficulties in designing and selecting these molecules with current tech.

Aficamten

  • Cytokinetics, Incorporated (CYTK).
  • Mechanism of Action: cardiac myosin inhibitor.
  • Indication: obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

Aficamten, developed by Cytokinetics, is a classic case of a significant best-in-class opportunity. The therapy is an oral inhibitor that is the next generation of therapy and will compete against Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) Camzyos, or mavacamten (~$700 million in revenues, growing fast). Unfortunately for BMY, aficamten has multiple benefits: a shorter half-life that allows for daily oral administration but a quicker dose adjustment phase; a far safer pharmacological profile due to reduced drug interactions; and likely higher efficacy due to the aforementioned characteristics. The mechanisms differ: while Camzyos binds to the cardiac muscle myosin catalytic site, aficamten binds to an allosteric site. Essentially, mavacamten prevents some myosin heads from being available to attach to actin fibers, while aficamten merely slows down the activity of myosin heads by preventing the release of phosphate. Both myosin-blocking drugs are set to disrupt the standard-of-care beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, and blood thinners.

Aficamten is yet another therapy that highlights the strategic excellence of targeting allosteric sites that lead to far less off-target effects than active site binding inhibitors. This is also a case to keep an eye on to see how small differences in efficacy or safety can be used to disrupt first-movers to a given indication or modality. There will be more about those ideas later.

Rilvegostomig

  • AstraZeneca.
  • Mechanism of Action: TIGIT and PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor mAb.
  • Indication: various cancers.

Merck & Co., Inc.'s (MRK) Keytruda was, and remains, one of the greatest therapies of all time due to the simple efficiency of checkpoint inhibition in strengthening the immune response to tumors. For over a decade, Keytruda has been the standard of care for numerous cancers, despite numerous competitors in the space. As we approach the Loss of Exclusivity in 2028, dozens of companies are attempting to position themselves to become the new standard-bearers of oncology. To this end, AstraZeneca’s rilvegostomig is attempting to provide increased efficacy with a bispecific antibody that targets both PD-1 and TIGIT. Their goal is to provide a dual-targeting mechanism that is superior to standard of care. To that end, AZN initiated 10 Phase III trials for this therapy to collect enough data to prove efficacy (although you must wait until ~2028 to find out).

The issue is that since Keytruda entered the market, all new checkpoint inhibitors must be compared to the stalwart therapy. This prevents some efficacious antibodies from being approved to prevent redundancy, protect patents, and justify new treatment options for patients. So far, rilvegostomig is a case of what can go wrong. TIGIT is yet to be a validated pathway, with no mAb approved yet. In fact, GSK plc (GSK) and Merck both ended their TIGIT antibody programs. The question now is whether rilvegostomig’s bispecific mechanism of action can prove superior through clinical trials that focus on high-quality patient selection. If successful, rilvegostomig will be the last survivor of the coy anti-TIGIT field and develop a significant niche. For those who want to believe, the success of Janssen from Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Genmab A/S (GMAB)/Merus N.V.'s (MRUS) Bizengri are initial indications that bispecifics can supplant a small niche within the empire of Keytruda. None, however, seems ready to seize the crown.

Ifininatamab Deruxtecan (I-DXd)

  • Merck and Daiichi Sankyo Company, Limited (OTCPK:DSKYF).
  • B7-H3 targeting antibody, first-in-class.
  • Indications: ES-SCLC, SCLC, pancancer.

I-DXd paints a similar picture to rilvegostomig as it is another oncological antibody seeking to provide significant increases in benefit for patients suffering from solid tumors. This time, the target is B7-H3, a new, unvalidated checkpoint. However, I-DXd is an example of an antibody drug conjugate, essentially a magic bullet that targets cancer cells expressing B7-H3 with a chemical that kills cells in that area. The ADC technology is already proven by the success of Daiichi Sankyo and Seagen, from Pfizer Inc. (PFE), and so now the current market strategy is to expand ADCs to new targets. The key is selecting which antibody should be used to aim at each target. I-DXd faces competition from traditional checkpoint inhibitors and enoblituzumab, an anti-B7-H3 antibody that was developed by MacroGenics, Inc. (MGNX).

Enoblituzumab was also an ADC at one point, but that program was cancelled as the company attempted to validate their own conjugate ligand. It is not the best choice to validate two mechanisms at once. I argue that Merck made the right choice investing in Daiichi’s technology, as it is proven, but it provides the opportunity to enter a new immuno-oncology niche via the new target pathway. So far, the data seems to support a winning strategy. This will lead to other biopharmas to change their strategy moving forward: using advanced but validated platforms when assessing the validity of new antibody targets. This is set to benefit Pfizer and Daiichi as the preeminent ADC IP holders. Therefore, while no single antibody will ever reach Keytruda’s highs, market share will be slowly eaten away by these superior but niche therapies. The real winners: patients and insurers, as the myriad of competitors and biosimilars enter the market at far lower prices as patent thickets fade away.

Alnodesertib

  • Artios (private).
  • Mechanism of Action: ATR inhibition.
  • Indication: ATM-negative cancer.

While we have detailed two high-impact antibody therapies for cancer treatment, do not count out innovation in small-molecule therapies. One of which, alnodesertib, represents a stunning new mechanism of action against cancers that have typically been untreatable. Essentially, alnodesertib inhibits the DNA repair protein ATR. Normally this would be unheard of as creating more damage to DNA typically leads to cancer progression. However, there are two main DNA repair proteins: ATR and ATM. When tumors are found to have ATM already inhibited naturally, an ATR inhibitor can cause both repair mechanisms to be disrupted entirely, leading to rapid cell death.

Alnodesertib is also the most advanced ATR inhibitor candidate as Bayer A.G. (OTCPK:BAYZF) and Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKKGY) ended their programs, primarily as Artios chose to focus on biomarker-driven patients in combination with low doses of chemotherapy. I hope this case is insightful in proving that there is no end to research and innovation, but some companies take a smarter approach than others. Make sure to view all pipeline candidates, no matter the company or modality, through this lens. This is particularly true when considering our next discussion: a three-way tie between novel immunology approaches.

An Immunology Tie: Amlitelimab, Zasocitinib, Icotrokinra

  • Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron/Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited (TAK) and Nimbus/JNJ and Protagonist Therapeutics, Inc. (PTGX).

Amlitelimab represents the disruptor: validation of a novel OX40 inhibition pathway. One major issue with current autoimmune therapies is that blocking the immune system leads to a lack of protection from pathogens. OX40 inhibition prevents this by blocking stimulatory pathways upstream of cytokine production and normalizes T-cell function without destroying the immune cells. Amlitelimab does have competition, rocatinlimab, but there is one key advantage: the effects of the therapy are long-lasting, allowing for amlitelimab dosing to be up to once every 12 weeks. The jury is out on whether OX40 is a valid target for various autoimmune diseases as current data suggest that the target may be less efficacious than biologics. However, 12-week dosing would be a key advantage for those with mild cases or in a second-line position. It will be up to management to make the most of these factors.

Zasocitinib represents the fast follower: an advanced allosteric inhibitor set to be best-in-class for TYK2 inhibition. TYK2 is already a promising target that has been validated with BMY’s Sotyktu, but zasocitinib shows superior allosteric binding that leads to increased efficacy and reduced side effects. Therefore, Takeda expects blockbuster revenues from this therapy despite the highly competitive and saturated market. Upon success, this molecule will be an essential confirmatory case in the ability of computational design to create best-in-class therapies, which is arguably far more important than the time to market (pattern recognition vs physics computation).

Icotrokinra represents the best-in-class potential: a first-in-class oral peptide therapeutic that inhibits IL-23. This is special as it offers biologic-like efficacy with safe, oral administration. As an IL-23 inhibitor, icotrokinra will compete against AbbVie Inc.'s (ABBV) Skyrizi, JNJ’s Tremfya, Sotyktu, zasocitinib and legacy JAK inhibitors. The potential is significant and may represent one of the next-generation megablockbusters. In fact, icotrokinra has even shown superiority to Sotyktu, so IL-23 inhibition may remain the leading target for various Th17 pathway diseases over TYK2. Of the group, icotrokinra has the most potential upon approval, but like always, it comes with some drawbacks for JNJ as Stelara and Tremfya sales will be cannibalized. However, once those patents expire, JNJ will have a likely mega-blockbuster that more than makes up for a few years of overlap.

Lonvoguran Ziclumeran

  • Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NTLA).
  • Mode of Action: CRISPR/Cas9 Gene Editing.
  • Indication: Hereditary Angioedema (HAE).

Yes, I am sure readers have been waiting for me to discuss gene editing. However, like CAR-T, I find the field is not mature enough. Also known as lonvo-z, Lonvoguran Ziclumeran is a therapy developed by Intellia Therapeutics that is the leading clinical candidate for in vivo CRISPR gene editing. Upon a successful approval, there will be two main implications. First, proof that lipid nanoparticles can be utilized for gene editing via CRISPR as issues with the amount of genetic material that can be released in a cell via a single particle have prevented administration via this path before. If successful, the proof that LNPs can deliver CRISPR to the liver (where lipid particles are processed) can provide validation for other CRISPR targets to be applied within the patient. This prevents the need for chemotherapy and cell transplantation as in the case with the approved blood therapies CRISPR Therapeutics AG's (CRSP) Casgevy and Bluebird's Lyfgenia that require marrow cell transplantation.

While lonvo-z is not competing against CRISPR Tx in SCC or B-Thal for this clinical candidate, the therapy proves that patient-friendly alternatives exist and will be developed soon. I see this as short-term bearish for CRSP and other ex vivo biotechs as patients will heavily consider waiting for a new delivery mechanism in the future. Primarily, this will be because investors would consider it a severe red flag if revenue or patient milestones are not met. Despite that, the picture remains unclear as recent reporting shows LNPs are still risky. Another Intellia trial, nexiguran ziclumeran for ATTR, was halted due to liver toxicity signals (although no deaths unlike Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.'s (SRPT) case).

All this does is prove how the gene editing field, while exciting, remains incredibly risky for the intermediate term until delivery mechanisms are improved. Invest across the landscape at your own risk. If you are interested in gaining exposure to this case’s indication of HAE, then perhaps take a look at the recent approval, Ionis’ antisense RNA therapy, donidalorsen.

Honorable Mentions

  • Cylembio

    • IO Biotech, Inc. (IOBT).

    • Cancer vaccine for melanoma.

    • Highlights the lack of efficacy of cancer vaccines (tumors have already evolved to avoid the immune response).

  • RP1 and 2

    • Replimune Group, Inc. (REPL).

    • Oncoviruses for advanced cancer therapy.

    • Unfortunately, I faced a CRL for RP1 in July.

  • Ivonescimab

    • Summit Therapeutics Inc. (SMMT) and Akeso, Inc. (OTCPK:AKESF).

    • Anti-PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody.

    • Approved in China, but the therapy may be at risk of failure in the US.

Conclusion

As a wide variety of technologies are approved and validated, biotechnology becomes more of a strategic chess match than a scientific endeavor. Making the right decisions is essential. I believe the therapies I discussed in this article highlight how small differences in a therapy can be just as influential and impactful as a new modality or novel pathway. As investors, we must assess the potential of each and every therapy and whether they will meet the demands that each company requires of them. Often, therapies fail to meet the mark, but some strategies outlined in this article point to indicators investors must look for when analyzing the potential of any therapeutic.

This is particularly necessary as GLP-1 agonists remain the primary outlay of investment based on the success of Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO). Those out of the loop require significant breakthroughs to keep up, as other mega opportunities remain lacking. Even for big pharma, an accumulation of poor decisions in the clinic may weigh on the fundamentals, as will be the case for poor Bristol Myers Squibb who faces the most competition from this list in aficamten vs Camzyos and zasocitinib/icotrokinra vs Sotyktu. I hope I provided some food for thought to go along with your Thanksgiving feasts.

Happy Holidays. Thanks for reading, and feel free to share your thoughts and clinical winners or losers in the comments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bullish: An Exchange Growing Faster Than The Market Notices
data.description:
Bullish (BLSH) is a cryptocurrency exchange, which debuted on the stock market on August 13 through its IPO at $37 initial price. The IPO was successful, highlighting a significant demand for the company's stock, reaching an intraday high of $118. The technology company has been trending down since the summer and now trades at roughly 7% premium versus its IPO price.

The blockchain technology company is a rapid top-line growth story. It has achieved roughly 80% revenue growth over the past year, implying a 9x outperformance versus peers and arguing for a rebound from the recent sell-off. Bullish is also tied to multiple tailwinds, including an existing possibility of a year-end rally, favorable crypto seasonality, pro-crypto administration, and others, which may elevate investor confidence and boost the stock price higher.

The company experiences a solid momentum in demand, and with cryptocurrency adoption and improved sentiment in the space, I think the business results may grow at a high double-digit pace. That is why I rate Bullish a Buy with a $57 price target over the next 12 months. This suggests a 39% upside possibility and broader market outperformance.

FQ3 2025: Top-Line Growth Remains Intact, A 33% Surge On A Quarter-Over-Quarter Basis

On November 19, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was a strong quarterly performance, underscoring improving fundamentals and increasing institutional demand. However, the market reacted to the results with roughly a 4% decline on the earnings day.

Bullish posted $76.50 million in revenue, approximately $5 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $0.10 in diluted EPS, in line with analysts' expectations.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that it has achieved $27 million in adjusted transaction revenue and $50 million in subscriptions, services, and other revenue. Moreover, Bullish has achieved $29 million in adjusted EBITDA and $18 million in net income. I would like to emphasize that these results are in line with or above what the company has previously guided, which justifies excellent execution and steady business performance.

BLSH launched crypto options on October 8, and this was an early success. The company partnered with 14 trading companies and has already surpassed $1 billion in options trading volume, indicating significant demand and seamless adoption. I think this pivot may unlock a new revenue growth cycle for the company.

Furthermore, management highlighted its indices AUM rose to $49 billion in FQ3 2025, a roughly 20% growth on a quarter-over-quarter basis from $41 billion. The company achieved above 100% new partner growth on a QoQ basis, and anticipates the trend to continue into the year-end.

I believe this highlight suggests strong institutional adoption and BLSH providing high-quality services. Such a substantial growth in new partners indicates a solid market demand for the company's infrastructure and rising interest in the cryptocurrency space. To that, in September 2025, BLSH secured a NY BitLicense, which entitles it to launch spot trading across US institutional investors. The company is laying the ground for its future success, and I think it is positioning itself for a market-leading position.

I would also like to emphasize that Bullish is a well-diversified business with diverse revenue streams. The company's focus on multiple areas guides a positive outlook, indicating a strengthening moat, as it specializes in both information services and market infrastructure spaces. This helps to gain additional revenue and visibility from operating streams, such as CoinDesk Data or Media, Events, and I think that may appear a compelling addition to its primary business of exchange, liquidity, and indices.

Furthermore, with a pro-crypto administration, a more favorable crypto environment, and a clearer legal framework, there is a likelihood that demand for cryptocurrencies may accelerate, and BLSH positions itself to benefit from such growth. For instance, President Trump signed the GENIUS Act, which establishes a framework for stablecoins and accelerates the adoption of digital assets. This supports my bullish thesis and a more favorable environment for crypto, and remains a tailwind for the company. A higher trading volume may lead to higher market infrastructure revenue.

To note, in FQ3 2025, the company achieved $142 billion in total trading volume, with spot purchases leading and accounting for $130 billion. Derivatives trading volume achieved $12 billion. Although I think spot trading is likely to remain a significant part of the total revenue portion further, the company's focus and strategic partnerships in the space of derivatives make me think derivatives volume may substantially accelerate. This would support a bullish narrative for the company.

To conclude, this has been a solid quarter for the company, underscoring a strong demand for its services, new partnerships driving the top-line growth, and cost efficiency measures supporting bottom-line expansion. Bullish positions itself to dominate the space, and with tailwinds tied to cryptocurrencies and digital asset adoption, the company appears to be well-positioned to benefit from favorable changes. I think it may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors favoring the crypto space and seeking exposure to the landscape through equity markets.

Bullish Appears Expensive, But If Future Tailwinds Materialize, It May Remain At The Current Valuation

Bullish currently trades at a forward P/E of roughly 145, representing a 13x overvaluation versus peers at an 11x earnings multiple. Although this may appear concerning, the blockchain technology company is a high-demand, high-growth name, and its future outlook supports a premium valuation.

The chart above indicates that Bullish has been used in trading at a significant earnings multiple over the past years. One could argue, the current valuation stands for a multi-year low, and the cryptocurrency exchange may appear to be well-positioned to rebound from this sell-off. This also signals there is room for multiple expansions if execution delivers.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 0.90, the company appears to be undervalued versus peers at 1.05. The 15% discount supports my thesis and argues for a bullish continuation.

On the other hand, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 22.85 and 22.15, respectively, imply 7x overvaluation at the midpoint versus sector medians of 3.15 and 2.91. Based on these metrics, it appears that the market anticipates significant revenue growth in the future, and if BLSH struggles to meet the expectations, the downside is not protected.

Furthermore, Bullish has a low-leveraged capital structure, and this supports an elevated multiple. The company has roughly $583 million in total obligations and $445 million in cash; thus, it covers the debt to a large extent. This is appealing, as management has financial flexibility to use leverage if needed.

As highlighted previously, the company has been an outstanding top-line growth story, so far. An 80% revenue growth remains significant and signals outperformance versus peers. In addition to this, typically Q4 is the most bullish calendar quarter for crypto, yet this year, Bitcoin is down roughly 24% so far, and Ethereum 29%. Both of these coins typically experience a significant double-digit growth in Q4; thus, historical data argue that December may spark positive investor confidence, which could support continued revenue growth for Bullish.

The last argument is also supported by digital asset treasury companies. There are more such publicly listed companies that are applying a HODL strategy for both Bitcoin and Ethereum, and provide a floor price. In addition, these companies are also gradually acquiring coins, and that may contribute to a positive outlook for the space.

Overall, I think Bullish is not a cheap stock, but its phenomenal growth and future tailwinds justify the valuation. The company experiences solid momentum in demand, remains a low-leveraged business, and the recent sell-off argues that sentiment may be overdone. Driven by future tailwinds, I think Bullish is well-positioned to rebound from these levels, and if crypto sentiment shifts, this could spark the end-of-year rally for equities with crypto exposure.

Wall Street analysts forecast $0.28 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $0.67 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I generally agree with the estimates, yet if crypto sentiment shifts and coins experience an all-time high run over the next months, the estimates may appear conservative. Nevertheless, I favor a conservative approach. Thus, if we apply an 85x earnings multiple, highlighting roughly 40% lower than the current forward P/E to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $0.67, we arrive at my price target of $57. I think the company is well-positioned to achieve the targets, and favorable tailwinds could spark repricing sooner, rather than later.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Bullish that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company remains heavily dependent on cryptocurrency sentiment and is sensitive to crypto market volatility. For instance, if the seasonality tailwind doesn't play out, this could pressure the company's performance. If Bitcoin and Ethereum experience a period of consolidation, trading volume is likely to decline, too, and investors may not be willing to pay a premium for BLSH.

Although the space is rapidly expanding, Bullish experiences fierce competition. Other well-known names, such as Kraken, Coinbase, Bybit, Binance, and others, are competing for the market share. If investors think Bullish is losing a position to its peers, repricing could lead to a stock price decline.

The crypto company is highly sensitive to regulatory uncertainty. Although the current administration is pro-crypto, any negative regulatory frameworks could erode the landscape's sentiment, and the stock may correct from these levels. On the other hand, this may also act as a significant tailwind, sparking the end-of-year rally.

I think that if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, Bullish remains well-positioned to rebound from a multi-year low valuation.

Conclusion: Bullish Is Positioning To Dominate The Space — A Buy

I believe Bullish may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking to expand crypto exposure via equity markets. Bullish is a rapidly growing cryptocurrency exchange and blockchain technology company that is tied to multiple tailwinds, outweighing the concerns.

Weak crypto seasonality performance highly contributed to the downside trend of the stock. However, with more DAT companies in place and pro-crypto administration, I think Bullish may rebound from these levels. The company continues expanding, justified by partnerships with trading platforms and the strategic launch of crypto options, which may drive customer growth.

The company is now trading at close to its IPO price, which may signal that the sell-off appears overdone. I rate Bullish a Buy with a $57 price target over the next 12 months. This represents a 39% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 11 Wall Street analysts imply a $53 price target, representing a 31% upside potential.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Giving Thanks For These Dirt Cheap Black Friday Bargains
data.description:
My favorite yield range for finding potential alpha-generating dividend investments is in the mid-to-high single-digit range. This is because stocks that yield this high do not need to generate more than mid-single-digit annualized growth rates to deliver double-digit annualized total returns, while at the same time not being so high-yielding that they implicitly bring with them extreme risk.

A mid-to-high single-digit growth rate is quite achievable for many companies because organic growth alone from the economy plus inflation typically gets you to that number, and if they retain any cash flows, they can reinvest them to further drive that growth rate higher. Thus, buying a quality business that yields at least 5% generally positions an investor extremely well to achieve double-digit annualized total returns over time. Additionally, if you can buy on a value basis, you get the valuation multiple expansion component, which can potentially further drive the total returns even higher. You can then overlay an opportunistic capital recycling strategy on top of that to even further accelerate the compounding process into the mid-to-high teens annualized total returns.

With this strategy in view, in today’s article, I am going to discuss two dividend growth stocks (SCHD) that yield between 5% and 9% which I think are very attractive after their recent sell-offs.

Black Friday Bargain #1

The first one I will talk about is a multifamily REIT (VNQ) called BSR Real Estate Investment Trust (OTCPK:BSRTF). It operates in Sunbelt markets with about 90% of its income coming from the Texas Triangle. This makes it a near pure play bet on quality multifamily real estate (VNQ) in the fastest-growing markets in Texas: Dallas, Austin, and Houston.

While these markets have recently been plagued by overbuilding, that cycle is starting to turn as they have finally generated blended rental spreads this past quarter for the first time in a while. Additionally, the company has recently completed a transformation process in which it has sold a bunch of older properties and recycled the proceeds into much younger properties that have significant lease-up capability. Moreover, as these properties become stabilized, management expects the transactions to be highly accretive while also improving the overall quality of its portfolio. The balance sheet also remains in fine shape, and the dividend yield has now reached almost 5%. On top of that, they pay out monthly distributions, and best of all, they trade at a huge discount to NAV.

While the REIT is battling lingering oversupply headwinds in its markets, the long-term future looks extremely bright for BSRTF due to the strong population growth tailwind that its target markets enjoy as businesses move from California, Chicago, and New York to markets like Texas and Florida due to a better business environment. Significant valuation multiple expansion potential also exists as the market eventually recognizes the deeply embedded private market value of its assets and the oversupply cycle turns into a more normalized environment where rent and same-store NOI growth accelerate as new supplies are absorbed by strong population growth. As a result, we think that the recent sell-off presents a very attractive opportunity for investors to load up on shares of this high-quality, Texas-focused, multifamily REIT.

Thus, BSRTF poses a similar investment thesis as the larger Sunbelt-focused multifamily REIT Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc. (MAA), but with greater concentration on Texas, a bit more leverage, a steeper discount to NAV, a higher dividend yield, and a smaller company size that makes it a more attractive buyout candidate.

Black Friday Bargain #2

The other attractive opportunity to buy on the recent dip is Hess Midstream LP (HESM). HESM is a midstream (AMLP) business that, despite its name, issues a 1099 tax form, making it potentially suitable for inclusion in retirement accounts. Note that this is not tax advice; it is just my own opinion based on my own experience, so be sure to do your own due diligence.

HESM is attractive because it offers a dividend yield of over 9% and has been growing its dividend at a very aggressive clip with a 5% annualized dividend per share growth target, plus additional dividend growth in line with the percentage of outstanding shares that HESM’s management has been buying back as part of its aggressive buyback program.

On top of the highly impressive shareholder capital return yield, HESM has several other things going for it. Firstly, its cash flows are backed by minimum volume commitment contracts. Therefore, it boasts one of the most stable cash flow streams in the midstream space. Additionally, some headwinds and uncertainty were recently lifted as BlackRock, Inc.'s (BLK) GIP fully exited its stake in HESM earlier this year, which had been a drag on the share price. Additionally, Chevron Corporation's (CVX) acquisition of Hess was finalized, which also removed some uncertainty that was providing some overhang on the stock. Thus far, CVX has shown an intent to continue HESM’s capital allocation program, which includes consistent dividend growth and fairly aggressive share repurchases. Additionally, CVX’s much higher credit rating strengthens HESM’s counterparty profile and helps to reduce some counterparty concentration risk, thereby earning HESM an upgrade to an investment-grade credit rating recently.

While the stock market initially cheered CVX’s constructive approach to managing HESM after it acquired Hess, the stock has since tumbled due to a combination of factors. Firstly, the broader midstream space has faced some headwinds recently due to persistently weak energy prices. Moreover, HESM released news that expectations for increased rig count in CVX’s Bakken assets have been trimmed, thereby leading to a flattish outlook for oil (USO) production in the basin, which is leading to lower growth potential for HESM.

However, the vast majority of HESM’s cash flows come from natural gas (UNG) contracts anyway, and the growth profile there remains solid. Additionally, due to reducing growth capex, HESM will still be generating a lot of cash flows despite the reduced growth guidance, which it can use to repurchase shares and grow its dividend, keeping the capital return profile very attractive for shareholders for the next few years at least.

Granted, the long-term outlook for HESM does remain somewhat iffy due to their heavy reliance on one region and one counterparty, but there is no evidence to suggest that an apocalyptic decline in production in the basin is anywhere near. HESM has a very low leverage ratio and a recent vote of confidence from the rating agencies, which instills a lot of confidence that a solid future is ahead for the company. Additionally, management continues to buy back stock aggressively and grow its dividend quite generously, which also signals confidence for the company’s future.

As a result, while it is certainly not the lowest-risk midstream opportunity out there today, the high yield and strong dividend growth rate, coupled with its highly contracted cash flow profile, low leverage ratio, and substantial buyback program, make it a very attractive risk-adjusted buy-the-dip opportunity, especially for those looking to avoid K-1 tax forms with a midstream investment.

Investor Takeaway

Between BSRTF and HESM, investors have the opportunity to buy very attractive dividend yields that also have solid dividend growth potential moving forward, backed by sound balance sheets, quality assets, and that trade at very attractive valuations after their recent sell-offs.

As a result, we have been buying both to add further diversification and upside potential in our 7% yielding dividend growth-oriented Core Portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brilliant Earth: The Dividend Rush Is Over, Now The Fundamentals Kick In
data.description:
Brilliant Earth’s (BRLT) stock went on a bull run, climbing fast before sliding right back down. That's the story in short.

This one is pretty easy to explain. The company dropped a special dividend of $0.25 per share with an ex date of August 22 and a payout on September 7. At a stock price of roughly $1.50, that meant a massive 16.6% yield overnight.

A lot of folks rushed into the stock, and the price shot up more than 77% right up until the day the dividend was paid.

I said it back in August, and it still holds up. Special dividends don’t just create long-term value out of nowhere.

The stock is now trading near $1.70, down around 35%. That leaves investors with a total return of roughly -11.5% since my last article, versus a gain of just 3% for the S&P over the same stretch.

What I expected is exactly what happened. The stock started to slide once management failed to lay out a dividend schedule and the market began to price it in as a one-off.

But before we peek at the quarter (where we finally saw some good developments with double-digit growth in engagement rings, Brilliant Earth’s most profitable line), let’s first consider how much of that FCF and cash pile could actually make its way back to shareholders.

What to Expect from the Returns?

If you've been watching, this stock has traded below its cash per share since the IPO. Just keep in mind, the way Brilliant Earth is owned isn't easy to figure out.

Like I said, there are four share classes.

Class A is the one you can trade publicly. It has the economic rights, but only one vote per share. Unlike it, Classes C and D have the right to ten votes per share, allowing the founders (Beth Gerstein and Eric Grossberg) to retain control of the company.

If we look at the fully diluted share count (since shares from other classes can be converted to Class A and obtain economic rights), we see approximately 100 million shares.

Just to get a sense, look at the cash per share based on Class A alone, and then see the picture with the ~85 million paired LLC units added in:

Through most of FY 2025, the stock remained close to fully diluted cash per share, and now it's sitting above it, pricing in some hefty returns down the road.

Since they repaid all the long-term debt, it's also interesting to look at the net debt per share:

Well, that just backs up what we said before.

To work out the potential return here, starting with share buybacks, there are a couple of things worth keeping in mind:

  1. The current program began at the end of FY 2023 and consisted of authorization to repurchase up to $20 million in shares with a deadline of December 8, 2026.
  2. So far, the program still has $18.9 million left for buybacks. Since launch, though, Brilliant Earth has only used $1.1 million of it. Not exactly a big return to shareholders.
  3. Class A outstanding shares are up by at least 2.7 million since the program began, driven by conversions from other share classes. That makes the goal pretty clear. This was a dilution management exercise more than a true capital return strategy.

At an average price of about $2 per share, that cash would be enough to withdraw around 9.45 million Class A shares. That alone would shrink the public float by roughly 62%, leaving just 5.7 million Class A shares outstanding, or close to 90 million on a fully diluted basis.

Since some pre-IPO investors, mainly private equity firms, still haven't sold their stakes, I see the buybacks being used mostly to prevent the small float from getting crushed as those shares get converted and sold in the market.

So I wouldn't expect much of a return from here.

Now, let's talk about the dividend.

Well, thinking rationally and looking at the clues in the air, I would say it would be a terrible idea for Brilliant Earth (a small-cap in a cyclical sector, like jewelry) to start paying quarterly dividends or distributing money through special dividends.

Still, if they have a strong Q4 in FY 2025 and keep the momentum going into FY 2026 and there's no M&A in sight, I wouldn't rule out another special dividend.

The odds aren't huge, but they're not zero either.

Looking at the new guidance (sales growth of 3 to 4.5% year-over-year and adjusted EBITDA margin of 2 to 3%), we could project an adjusted EBITDA between $8.7 and $13.2 million. Since their EBITDA to OCF conversion rate is typically ~2x EBITDA (due to SBC), we could expect a midpoint OCF of ~$22 million.

If capex comes in at approximately $6 million, FCF would be $16 million.

Let's assume they continue diluting minority shareholders through SBC and conversions (17 to 20 million Class A shares until FY 2026). If they wanted to pay an additional $0.25 per share, the total amount to be distributed would be approximately $5 million, fully covered by FCF.

It's not an ideal setup for a growth company, but they could pull more special dividends out of the hat if they wanted to.

Of course, dilution is a problem that will continue to persist due to both conversions and SBC, and any dividend that comes will be to compensate for that and not to provide a source of income for minority shareholders. Well, at least not for now.

Q3 In a Nutshell

First up, management says the jewelry space sits around $350 billion, which already tells you a lot.

You really didn’t think I’d let that one slide, right?

Reading between the lines, that puts Brilliant Earth at roughly 0.12% of the entire market. This fits perfectly with what the CEO said about it being a highly fragmented market.

As one of the largest stand-alone jewelers, we are uniquely positioned to continue challenging the status quo, capturing market share and leading the transformation of the highly fragmented $350 billion jewelry industry

For comparison, Signet Jewelers (SIG), one of the biggest players out there, controls nearly 2% of the market.

With that kind of cash on hand and an asset-light setup, Brilliant Earth is actually in a pretty comfortable spot to play consolidator if the right small-cap names come along (as I told you in my first article here).

Back to the quarterly numbers they finally pulled off double-digit revenue growth.

Most of the $110.3 million came from a 45% year-over-year surge in fine jewelry and engagement rings finally started picking up again. Overall orders rose 17% year-over-year with repeat purchases up around 16%

It is worth peeling back the layers on this one.

You can clearly see fewer and fewer households being led by couples, especially among Millennials and Gen Z. This trend actually bottomed out about three years ago (marriage rate of 46.8%) and is now slowly working its way back toward pre-pandemic levels over the next couple of years.

But that's not the whole story. Research also shows the average price of engagement rings has been sliding and now sits around $6,500.

These aren’t small bumps in the road for Brilliant Earth.

You don’t have to look far to spot the effect. Average order value has cooled off by a mid-single-digit rate and now hovers around $2,209.

Now let’s talk about margins. Even with platinum and gold flirting with record highs, management still kept gross margin north of 50%, right in line with their long-term target.

Just take a look at this:

Brilliant Earth’s biggest enemy to profitability is still SG&A.

To be fair, they're trying to fix it. The CEO even pointed out a 300 bp improvement in marketing efficiency on the last earnings call.

We achieved remarkable marketing efficiency, driving 300 basis points of year-over-year marketing leverage while still increasing traffic and delivering double-digit revenue growth

Still, when you stack them up against other jewelry companies, they’re clearly behind.

Brilliant Earth Continues With Few Catalysts on the Table

As I told you before, I still think that an M&A could put Brilliant Earth back in the growth stock category. Something similar to what happened with LuxExperience (LUXE) when it merged MyTheresa with YOOX-NET-A-PORTER.

But aside from that—even though I still believe in perhaps another special dividend-driven rally—I don't see much that could move Brilliant Earth from where it is.

Doubtful? Let's take another look at the multiples here.

Remember, I had FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA pegged between $8.7 and $13.2 million.

Once you run that through the revenue line, you’re looking at something very close to a 1% EBITDA margin. In this case, we could expect an EBITDA of ~$4.5 million, a multiple of 13.1x.

Is this cheap? Definitely not the multiple you would expect for a company that is growing slowly.

Looking ahead to FY 2026, Wall St. expects revenue of $456.2 million. I see significant upside here if Brilliant Earth returns to profitability in line with its average over the past 5 years.

Think about it. If they manage to hit something close to a 5% EBITDA margin (that's around $22.8 million EBITDA) and a clean balance sheet, an 8.3x multiple would put the stock around $2.20.

Maybe one day they'll earn a richer multiple, but right now it's still something they need to show in the numbers. The Chinese company Chow Tai Fook (OTCPK:CJEWY), for example, trades at slightly higher multiples (almost 11x EBITDA), but its EBITDA is growing in the low single digits.

With EBITDA likely sitting somewhere between 1 and 3% while they fix their marketing spend, I see the stock hovering a little under $2 within a fair range.

That said, I reiterate my 'Hold' rating here.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Coverage Of Fat 11.3% Dividend Yield Expands
data.description:
Blackstone Secured Lending Fund. (BXSL) is paying out a well-covered near-record dividend while trading at a rare discount to net asset value ("NAV") that will prove transient if the Fed were to make one final December 25 basis point cut to keep rates at 3.50% to 3.75% for much of 2026. The core risk faced by BXSL shareholders was a pending dividend cut in the event that coverage were to slip below 100% for a sustained period. This coverage dipped by around 8 basis points sequentially to exactly 100% during the second quarter, with the near-term specter for BXSL shareholders coalescing around a third-quarter coverage dip below 100%. This didn't happen, with BXSL generating net investment income ("NII") of $0.82 per share, a beat of 4 cents per share on consensus estimates to provide 106% coverage for the base dividend.

BXSL last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.77 per share, unchanged from its prior distribution, and $3.08 per share annualized for an 11.3% dividend yield. The yield is fat versus BXSL's historical range, but has compressed in recent weeks from a record high as investors moved to lock in positions in BDCs that had moved to trade at more substantial discounts to NAV. BXSL reported a third-quarter NAV of $27.15 per share, a sequential dip of 18 cents from the second quarter, but with the ticker now trading hands at a small 0.3% discount to third-quarter NAV per share. This discount was as deep as 8.5% against BXSL's 52-week low. The probability of the Fed funds rate being cut by another 25 basis points has slimmed down from a 56% probability in the third week of October to 22%, paving the way for BDCs to face a less hostile rate environment from next year after the pending December cut. I last covered BXSL in October.

Originations, Credit Quality, And Dividend Coverage

Total investment income came in at $358.6 million, up year-over-year by 4.5% and beating consensus estimates by $5.2 million. Growth was driven by a significant ramp in originations during the third quarter. Investment commitments came in at $1.29 billion during the quarter, with investment fundings at $1 billion. This formed the highest quarter for originations since the fourth quarter of 2024. Positive cadence with originations while maintaining strong credit quality and with a more muted outlook for rate cuts forms the most salient pathway for BDCs to avoid a race to the bottom of falling NII, dragging down dividend coverage, and forcing dividend cuts. Net funded investment activity after adjusting for investments sold and repaid was positive at $571 million, with BXSL set to maintain this positive growth into its fourth quarter. Critically, loans on non-accrual status at the end of the quarter stood at 0.1% of BXSL's portfolio at cost, a dip from 0.3% in the second quarter and much lower than its BDC peers.

The upswing in BXSL's origination momentum was implied during the last quarter earnings call when management flagged that they expect deal activity to pick up in the second half of the year. The recent quarter represented a reversal of what had been three consecutive quarters of NII and dividend coverage declining. Hence, while the dip in third-quarter NAV per share was a setback to management efforts to maintain and grow the BDC's intrinsic value, covering the dividend bodes well for BXSL's future NAV per share growth and sets the BDC up to maintain distributions in the event the Fed doesn't cut rates through most of 2026, or at least in the rate-setting meetings before Powell is replaced at the end of May.

The dip in NAV per share was led by a $0.25 per share net realized and unrealized loss, partially countered by a 2 cents per share gain from share issuances at a premium to NAV from the BDC's at-the-market offering program. BXSL is set to move back into a solid premium as the market prices in a more constricted pathway for rate cuts beyond December. Hence, we could see NAV per share and NII grow in the fourth quarter if BXSL continues to expand origination volume while credit quality remains healthy.

Risks To Bulls

BXSL's coverage of the dividend remains tight, with the BDC paying out around 94% of NII to shareholders. This is above the 90% requirement for BDCs. This does ramp up the risk profile of a cut if the Fed were to be more aggressive on rate cuts next year than current market expectations, and we see a deterioration of credit conditions on poor economic growth. Shareholders need to be cognizant of this narrow coverage and the volatility this will bring on a quarter-over-quarter earnings basis as the market tries to guess which BDCs will see dividend cuts and which will be safe.

Conclusion

BXSL is likely set to see its dividend coverage continue to expand, pushing through a recovery of its common shares back to a premium over NAV per share to allow for the BDC to resume accretive share offerings. However, my bullish case hinges on one more rate cut, then an indefinite pause for at least the first half of 2026. BXSL is being rated as a buy.

data.title: Modiv Industrial: Monthly Dividend REIT With Potential From Tariffs And Supply Chain Shifts
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Modiv Industrial, Inc. (MDV) is the only direct-to-retail crowdfunded REIT to ever publicly list on a stock exchange, with 43 properties leased to several mission-critical tenants across industries including infrastructure, automotive, defense, industrials, government and technology.

The stock has been quite volatile since the IPO in 2022, although it’s still under pressure since the initial tariffs announcement, despite their business model of focusing on single-tenant net-lease industrial manufacturing real estate, currently presenting an opportunity to get solid monthly dividend yields.

MDV’s latest report has been solid, with $4.511 million in AFFO and an increase to $0.36 per share vs $0.34 per share a year ago. What’s much more impressive is the YTD growth, with the $13.2 million AFFO reached over the first 9 months of the year representing $1.07 per share, meaning a massive ~10.31% increase on a per share basis.

This comes in part thanks to cost savings and asset recycling that’s advancing, and we should also consider the disposition of a Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) property, whose lease expired at the end of July 2025 and is currently held for sale at a contractual value of ~$25.6 million, with an agreement with KB Home (KBH) expected to close by December 15, with the possibility of an extension to February 15. They also expect to do a parcel split and sell a Californian property formerly leased to Solar Turbines whose lease expired on September 30, 2025. These should represent very solid sources of capital, although it can take some time until they’re reflected again in the AFFO.

The company’s WALT (weighted average lease term) is also very solid, with just 1% of their leases expiring under 2 years, only ~8% over the next 2-5 years and half of them expiring in 15 years or more, so we’re looking at a very solid 14.2-year average overall, with ~2.5% average annual rent increases. They even expect this WALT to grow as they recycle assets, so it's definitely a very big plus.

Financially, based on MDV’s latest report, we see a normal position for a REIT, without a lot of cash available but with significantly more assets than liabilities, with a normal amount of leverage. Even when we exclude the noncontrolling interests, the current market cap is not far from their ~$164.82 million in equity, and we should keep in mind that we’re talking about assets that likely went up in value since being recorded on the balance sheet, not depreciated.

It’s also important to note that their $250 million term loan (most of their debt) is due in 2027, so they have a bit of time to wait for rate cuts to advance before having to refinance—potentially splitting this into more loans and spreading it over more time.

They also failed to approve a reverse split of anywhere between 1:500 to 1:1,500, which was supposed to be followed by a forward split (these do not include their preferred (MDV.PR.A) 7.375% Series A stock, currently paying less than the common stock). The CEO detailed the reasoning behind it very well in this news release, basically saying that this was supposed to be just an administrative function intended to reduce costs and potentially increase liquidity.

This is something that goes back to the company’s crowdfunding era, and it’s one of the reasons the CEO even joined in 2018. As you probably guessed from this, one of the main reasons behind this move was to cut costs with the transfer agent, as the company had nearly 10,000 separate accounts when going public, compared to a handful that a normal company does (brokers acting as intermediaries), as they got a lot of people to join the crowdfunding initially. This generates expenses in the six-digit range every year, and it’s an interesting thing to remember about smaller companies that’s often ignored.

At the moment the release was published, the company had about 4,000 accounts at their transfer agent, of which about 3,500 of these held less than 1,500 shares (a lot under 500 even), meaning a lot of unnecessary costs since they aren’t allowed to cash out accounts with more than 1 share. What this reverse split was supposed to do is allow them to cash out these “dormant” accounts that still didn’t move to a broker (basically buy out some of the people who bought during the crowdfunding and perhaps even forgot about the account), eliminate unnecessary costs, and then do a split back to normal share prices.

Unfortunately, the measure missed the necessary threshold by a small margin, despite a wide majority of the people who voted being in favor of it (not enough votes overall), but maybe another try could work next time.

Dividends & Buybacks

Modiv pays a monthly dividend (as the name also hints at) of $0.0975 per share, for a yield of roughly 8% today, which is among their highest in quite some time. This should cost them about $14.84 million based on the current number of shares, meaning a roughly 84.32% payout ratio on the common dividends based on an annualized AFFO of $17.6 million.

On March 4, 2025, Modiv also authorized a buyback program for its Series A preferred stock, and through September 30, 2025, it repurchased a total of 275,000 shares, meaning ~13.8% of the preferred shares issued, for a total of $6.5 million at an average cost of $23.74 per share.

Risks & Opportunities

One big opportunity to keep in mind for Modiv actually comes from Trump’s tariffs. As I mentioned before, I see them as a tool to get leverage in near/medium-term negotiations rather than a long-term norm, with the $750 billion LNG deal with the EU that came from them being a perfect example. However, another effect I expect is reinforcing the US’s internal supply chains, as companies would seek to prevent/avoid these tariffs and focus on local logistics and sourcing rather than relying on other countries.

Although the market initially panic-sold this like any other stock back in early April when tariffs were officially announced, the stock quickly jumped to nearly $17 later on the same month, although it retreated quickly after. Owning this kind of mission-critical, niche/specialized buildings that is essential for the supply chain is ultimately what the company does, and as companies bring back their production capacity inside the country, more opportunities for MDV should arise.

But again, this relative weakness we’ve seen for REITs is ultimately normal in this environment. As a REIT, the company depends on external capital to expand since they have to pay most of what they make back to us. As we can see, Modiv has maintained their debt around the same level since 2023, although they issued some stock back in early and late 2024 to fund some acquisitions with initial cap rates of ~8%. This is key for REITs since they don’t have that many sources of capital except for issuing stock, issuing debt or selling assets to acquire others, and weakness should be expected when the stock is trading at unattractive multiples and debt costs are high.

In fact, this external capital even goes back to the company’s inception, when they started raising capital from individual investors back in 2012 through crowdfunding.

This is why rate cuts will also be an essential catalyst for this asset class’ recovery. Not only would they make debt costs cheaper and encourage higher investments, but they could also boost the economy by increasing discretionary budgets, helping Modiv’s tenants and making the overall investment more attractive.

To simplify this, think about how the company would fund an investment with an initially expected 8% cap rate (normal in the industry, similar deals seen recently). They can issue stock, but that also costs them 8% per year (the current yield), while debt could be a bit cheaper but not significantly better due to the high interest rates affecting the SOFR rate, so they don’t have that many options to efficiently develop, unlike a low-rate environment where REITs trade at lower yields and debt is cheaper. This is a key reason why the asset class is not very attractive for the market during bad times, although I’d argue that this is the best time to consolidate a position at great yields.

On top of that, they can lower treasury yields given enough time, assuming that nothing fundamental changes for the US that would warrant borrowing at higher costs. As the theoretical risk-free rate would then fall, the market would be “forced” to move into better risk-adjusted yields, with Modiv being a solid contender for that especially if their cost of debt would fall as well. Even recently, the yield on the 10-year treasury fell below 4% but then jumped to nearly 4.2% due to the uncertainty around the next cuts before falling to the 4.04% seen today as the market’s fear slowed down a bit.

But again, these things are not instant. The correlation between rates and 10-year treasuries can be seen with the naked eye, but we can also notice the significant near-term volatility, so don’t expect this to solve everything overnight. I say that especially after the US Government went through its longest shutdown in history, which made the near-term future of these cuts questionable by the market, although some Fed officials signaled they see the possibility of a December cut—but we can’t be sure until it happens.

The CEO actually had a nice comment regarding these delayed effects and broader market moves during the Q3 Earnings Release:

But if we start to see real easing and we start to see some consistent trends for REITs, I don't know if that means we need a consolidation on the sort of the rest of the S&P and NASDAQ to get that or not, it's hard to say because you could argue that until tech and some of these names cool off, then no one is really going to ever consider boring REITs. But at the same time, if they force correct, is that just going to drag everyone back down. So it remains to see, it's pretty cloudy. But even despite that cloudiness, I feel pretty optimistic about what I'm seeing.

Another near-term catalyst in this regard could come from the new Fed Chair, with Powell’s term ending in May 2026 and Trump likely naming a more obedient Chair this time. Sure, that’s just one vote (the Federal Open Market Committee is composed of 12 votes), but as we’ve seen, especially in recent years, the Chair remains a very important spokesperson for the market, with immense influence that can give the market a lot of fear or confidence. I also later saw that the CEO even mentioned something regarding this during their Q3 Earnings Release, noting how the company has been public during quite a terrible environment, I might add.

If you think about the timing of this company’s IPO, we had the war in Ukraine coming a few days after, followed by rate hikes, so we haven’t seen them trade in a “normal” environment so far, with the stock being pressured by macro factors for basically all of its short existence.

It’s also worth noting that the CEO has a good history of mergers and acquisitions, and Modiv itself could be part of either of those, offering a solid portfolio of companies that could attract a potential buyer wanting to diversify while also trading at a size that allows them to carefully pick every single property they invest in—an advantage over larger companies that buy entire portfolios at once in billion-dollar transactions.

So, even though the long-term should look quite good for Modiv and its asset class, we should be aware that the near-term will likely continue to be volatile, which is not great for a company with inherently quite limited liquidity. This is why the management will have to continue being careful with their capital allocation, recycling, and issuing. Although I’d say they've done a great job so far, being such a small REIT also means that mistakes or tenant issues can be very costly, with a lot of dependency on California, a small property count, and even fewer tenants.

Valuation

Starting with an annualized AFFO of $17.6 million in 2025, I believe we can see them deliver a relatively solid CAGR of at least 7.5% over the first 5 years thanks to asset recycling moves, potential cost savings, rate cuts lowering interest expenses, followed by a 4.5% CAGR as the company still has a lot of room to develop, with a 3% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 8% (above my estimated 7.88% based on an ~8.5% cost of equity, ~7.5% cost of debt), $269.8 million in net debt, and 12.686 million shares and units outstanding as of the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $447.04 million, an equity value of $177.24 million, and an intrinsic value of $13.97 per share, which is almost identical to the current price.

Conclusions

I rate MDV a Buy thanks to their high-yield monthly dividends and potential recovery in an improving environment, with solid long-term growth potentially coming from macro tailwinds started by tariffs. Although MDV trades around a fair value based on my calculations, we’re talking about a fair value for a relatively high expected yield, while rate cuts should push these expectations lower and likely result in higher stock prices. Risks remain, especially due to their tenant concentration and property count, but that also comes with higher growth potential compared to REITs that are already virtually omnipresent.

data.title: Q3 2025 Insights From Gabelli Value Portfolio Managers
data.description:
Amid continued geopolitical tensions, inflation concerns, a still uncertain global trade environment, and a looming U.S. government shutdown at quarter-end, equity markets continued their seemingly inexorable climb in the third quarter. The S&P 500 Index rose 8.1% and small caps, which have long lagged the overall market, rose even more, with the Russell 2000 Index climbing 12.4%.

Artificial intelligence continues to be the primary driver of market returns, with most of the “Magnificent Seven” at or near all-time highs. That said, this quarter’s market rally has broadened, with cyclicals such as industrials and financials also contributing to the rally.

We wrote in our second quarter letter about the “Three T’s”: Trade, Taxes, and Trust. Trade continues to be in flux, with the U.S. signing deals with many partners (including the UK and European Union), though uncertainty remains for many significant countries, including China and India. Tax cuts were mostly extended in the One Big Beautiful Bill signed in July, which also provided additional stimulus with accelerated depreciation. But trust remains shaky, with an unpredictable administration, $37 trillion in national debt, and a $2 trillion annual budget deficit, leading gold and bitcoin to extend their rallies at nearly $4,000 per ounce and $120,000, respectively, as of this writing.

Another “T” we would add this quarter is “Treasuries.” The Federal Reserve cut rates for the first time this year in September, lowering the Fed Funds rate 25 bps to 4%-4.25%, with expectations for further cuts to come. Those additional cuts depend on the economy, inflation, and unemployment. Second quarter GDP grew a robust 3.8%, with estimates for third quarter GDP growth in the 1.5%-2% range. Inflation is well below its recent peak, though it actually rose to 2.9% in August from 2.7% in June and July – all still above the Fed’s 2% target. Unemployment, while still low at 4.3%, has also ticked up in recent months. 10-Year U.S. Treasury yields declined during the quarter and are currently around 4.1%. All things being equal (which they never are), lower rates should be good for equity prices as they support higher multiples and encourage investment and deal-making.

Deals, Deals & More Deals

Despite a lull around “Liberation Day” due to tariff uncertainty, the merger & acquisition boom expected under President Trump has come to fruition so far in 2025, with global deals up 33% year-to-date to $3 trillion, a four-year high. Private equity-backed M&A is up 27%. Continued lower interest rates, greater trade certainty, lower regulatory scrutiny, and a growing economy should further bolster deal activity.

Conclusion

Despite uncertainty on many macroeconomic and geopolitical fronts, there are some clear tailwinds for equities: easing trade tensions, lower rates, a lighter touch on regulation, and stimulative policies. That said, the market isn’t “cheap” at nearly 30x 2025 earnings. We continue to seek unappreciated and undervalued companies – often in the overlooked small to mid-cap area – to find quality franchise companies with catalysts that can surface value. Despite the market rally, opportunities do abound, and our team of portfolio managers and analysts continue to use our bottom-up process and Private Market Value with a Catalyst philosophy to find them.

Notes and Important Information

Premium/Discount Discussion

As a refresher for our shareholders, the price of a closed-end fund is determined in the open market by willing buyers and sellers. Closed-End Fund shares trade on an exchange and may trade at a premium to (higher than) net asset value (the market value of the Fund’s underlying portfolio and other assets less any liabilities) or a discount to (lower than) net asset value. Ideally, a Fund’s market price will generally track the NAV. However, a Fund’s premium or discount to NAV may vary over time.
